programs.name: Utah Medicaid Program

eligibility.income_limits: Varies by program (2025-2026 figures): Institutional/Nursing Home Medicaid: No strict limit, but most income pays toward care with $45 personal needs allowance[1][2][5]. HCBS Waivers: $2,901/month (New Choices Waiver) or $1,255-$1,305/month (Aging Waiver)[1][5]. Regular Medicaid (ABD): $1,255/month single or $1,704/month couple[1][5]. Medically Needy (Spenddown): $1,330/month single or $1,803.33/month couple (effective 3/1/26-2/28/27)[2]. No household size table specified beyond couples; applies to low-income elderly.
eligibility.asset_limits: Single applicant: $2,000 countable assets[2][4][5]. Married couples: $3,000-$4,000 combined if both apply; community spouse keeps up to 50% of assets ($157,920 max or $31,584 min if lower) if one applies[5]. Exempt: Primary home (equity ≤$730,000 if intent to return), one vehicle, personal belongings/household items/appliances, burial plots/irrevocable trusts ≤$7,000, life insurance ≤$1,500 face value[1][5].
other_requirements: Utah resident and U.S. citizen/qualified immigrant[1]
Blind, disabled, or nursing home level of care (NHLOC) for long-term care programs[1][2]
Functional need for ADLs in some programs[2]

benefits.value: Long-term care: Nursing home care, HCBS waivers (home/community-based services like daily living assistance), assisted living, adult foster care, non-medical home supports; regular ABD covers health services for aged/blind/disabled[1][2]. No specific dollar amounts/hours stated; covers as long as eligible, potentially lifelong[4].

methods: Online: https://medicaid.utah.gov/apply/[3] (inferred from official site structure)
In-person: Local offices (e.g., Utah/Wasatch/Summit Counties via MAG: 801-526-9675)[8]
Mail: Not specified; use online/paper forms via official site
application.processing_time: Coverage may begin 3 months prior to approval; applications sometimes take longer than expected—ask for usual timeframe[4]
application.waitlist: Not mentioned in sources; may vary by waiver demand[null]
documents: Proof of income, assets, residency, citizenship/immigration status, medical/functional needs documentation (e.g., for NHLOC)[1][2]

geography.regional_variations: Local offices/providers may differ (e.g., Utah/Wasatch/Summit Counties have specific contacts like 801-526-9675); potential wait time/provider variations not detailed[8]
offices_or_providers: Utah County/Wasatch/Summit: 801-526-9675, 801-526-0950[8]

gotchas: Multiple program tiers (Institutional, HCBS Waivers like Aging/New Choices, Regular ABD) with different income/asset rules—must select correct one[1][2]
Home exempt but subject to Estate Recovery after death[5]
Most income goes to care costs in nursing home program, only $45 personal allowance[1][2][5]
Medically Needy Spenddown for those over limits via high medical expenses[2]
Spousal impoverishment protections for community spouse assets/income[5]
programs.data_shape_notes: Multiple sub-programs with varying income caps by waiver type (e.g., Aging Waiver lower than New Choices); no strict income limit for nursing home but pay-down required; asset exemptions detailed but home equity capped at $730,000; medically needy spenddown pathway unique

programs.name: Utah PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)

eligibility.income_limits: No income limits; no financial criteria considered for eligibility
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits; financial eligibility not required
other_requirements: Live in the service area of a PACE organization (none currently in Utah)
Certified by the state as requiring nursing home level of care (NFLOC)
Able to live safely in the community with PACE services
Not enrolled in Medicare Advantage, Medicare prepayment plan, Medicare prescription drug plan, or hospice

benefits.value: Comprehensive medical and social services including primary care, nursing, therapies, caregivers, social workers, medications, and long-term care; no deductibles or copays for covered services; specific hours or amounts not detailed in sources

programs.official_source: No Utah-specific .gov source; general PACE info at https://www.cms.gov/medicare/medicaid-coordination/about/pace
gotchas: Utah does not currently offer PACE; one of 19 states without a program
No local providers or service areas exist in Utah
Families cannot apply; program unavailable despite national eligibility criteria
Other Utah programs (e.g., C-PACE for property, SLCC PACE scholarship) are unrelated
programs.data_shape_notes: Program not available in Utah; no providers, applications, or regional variations; national PACE model has no financial tests but requires service area proximity which does not exist here

programs.name: Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI)

eligibility.income_limits: Utah follows federal standards with income disregards. For 2026 (effective April 2026): QMB: Individual $1,350/month, married couple $1,824/month (at or below 100% FPL + $20 disregard). SLMB: Above QMB up to 120% FPL (individual ~$1,620, couple ~$2,189). QI: Above SLMB up to 135% FPL (individual ~$1,823, couple ~$2,462). Exact limits from prior years (2024): QMB individual $1,325/couple $1,783; SLMB individual $1,585/couple $2,135; QI individual $1,781/couple $2,400. Verify current FPL at state agency as announced yearly[2][3][6].
eligibility.asset_limits: Federal limits apply: $9,950 individual/$14,910 couple (2026); prior $9,660/$14,470. Countable: cash, bank accounts, stocks (over $1,500 face value), non-exempt life insurance. Exempt: primary home, one vehicle, household items, wedding/engagement rings, burial plots, up to $1,500 burial expenses, $20/$65 + half wages income disregard, food stamps, certain Native American payments[3][4][6].
other_requirements: Must be eligible for Medicare Part A (QMB) or enrolled in Part A (SLMB/QI); Part B required for SLMB/QI.
Utah resident.
QI: Cannot receive Medicaid[2].

benefits.value: QMB: Pays Part A premiums (if applicable), Part B premiums/deductibles, Part A/B coinsurance/copayments/deductibles. SLMB: Part B premiums only. QI: Part B premiums only. QMB issues Medicaid card ('MEDICARE COST-SHARING ONLY' if no other Medicaid); no card for SLMB/QI[1][2][6].

methods: Contact Utah Medicaid agency: https://medicaid.utah.gov/medicare-cost-sharing-programs/[2].
Phone or request application from state Medicaid office (specific local numbers via site).
Mail, phone, or in-person at local Medicaid offices.
application.processing_time: QMB: Up to 45 days, effective first of month after determination. SLMB/QI: Up to 3 months retroactive[1][2].
application.waitlist: QI: Possible when federal funds exhausted (not entitlement); no new applicants until next year[2].
forms: Application requested from Utah Medicaid agency (no specific form number listed)[1][2].
documents: Proof of income (pay stubs, SSI awards).
Proof of resources/assets.
Medicare card/benefit verification.
ID, residency proof.
Bank statements, asset valuations.

geography.restrictions: None; available statewide for eligible Medicare beneficiaries.
geography.regional_variations: No regional differences noted; uniform via state Medicaid[2].
offices_or_providers: Local Utah Medicaid offices (find via https://medicaid.utah.gov/)[2]

gotchas: QI not entitlement; funds can run out, blocking new applicants[2].
Income limits update April yearly with FPL; use $20 disregard for QMB[1][4].
Must have Part A enrolled for SLMB/QI, eligible for QMB[2].
QMB card needed to prevent provider billing; providers can't charge QMB beneficiaries[2].
Assets include most non-exempt items; exemptions often missed (e.g., one car, home)[3][4].
SLMB/QI no card issued; premium paid directly[2].
programs.data_shape_notes: Tiered by income brackets (QMB ≤100% FPL, SLMB 100-120%, QI 120-135%); QI funding capped; asset test with standard federal exemptions; statewide uniform

programs.name: Utah SNAP (Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program)

eligibility.income_limits: For households with a member 60+ or disabled (common for elderly), no gross income limit in Utah. Must meet net income limit only (net income = gross minus deductions like medical expenses over $35/month for elderly/disabled, shelter costs, utilities). Federal reference: $15,060 annual net for 1 person, $20,440 for 2 (Oct 2025-Sept 2026); exact net varies by deductions and household size[1][2][3].
eligibility.asset_limits: Households with member 60+ or disabled: $4,500 countable resources (e.g., bank accounts). Exempt: home, vehicles, retirement savings, household goods, life insurance cash value (if not income-producing), primary residence[2][6].
other_requirements: Household includes those who live together, buy/prepare food together (include spouse/children under 22 even if separate)[2]
U.S. citizenship not required; many non-citizens qualify if lived in U.S. 5+ years, children under 18, or receiving disability benefits[4][5]
No work requirements for seniors 60+[4][5]
ESAP for elderly/disabled: simplified process[4]

benefits.value: Monthly EBT card for food purchases; amount based on net income, household size, deductions (e.g., example 2-person elderly household: $415/month after calculation)[3].

methods: Online: mycase.utah.gov (primary Utah portal, inferred from state Workforce Services)[4]
Phone: 1-866-526-3663 (Utah SNAP hotline, standard state contact)
In-person: local Workforce Services offices (statewide)
Mail: Download form from jobs.utah.gov and mail to local office
application.processing_time: ESAP for elderly: one interview, approved for 3 years; standard processing 30 days[4].
documents: Proof of income (Social Security, pension, VA benefits)
Proof of expenses (medical >$35/mo for elderly, rent/mortgage, utilities)
ID, proof of residency
Bank statements for assets if near limit

geography.regional_variations: Uniform statewide via Utah Dept. of Workforce Services; local offices handle intake but no noted differences in rules/wait times[4][8].
offices_or_providers: Local Utah Workforce Services offices (e.g., Salt Lake County office for that area)[8]

gotchas: Elderly households skip gross income test but must pass net income; high medical/shelter costs can deduct significantly[1][2]
Assets $4,500 for elderly (higher than $3,000 standard); exempt items often missed (home, cars OK)[6]
Include all who buy/prepare food; non-citizens may qualify[2][5]
ESAP simplifies recertification to 3 years for elderly/disabled[4]
Only ~half of eligible seniors apply[1]
programs.data_shape_notes: No gross income test for households with 60+ member; higher $4,500 asset limit; ESAP 3-year approval with single interview; benefits calculated via net income + elderly-specific deductions (medical/shelter)[2][4][6]

programs.name: Utah LIHEAP (Low-Income Home Energy Assistance Program)

eligibility.income_limits: Gross monthly household income at or below 150% of the Federal Poverty Level. Specific limits from one source: 1 person $1,956; 2 people $2,644; 3 people $3,331; 4 people $4,018; 5 people $4,707; 6 people $5,394. Other sources cite examples like family of four at $3,750-$4,125/month.[1][3][6]
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits mentioned in state program sources; tribal program considers resources but not detailed for state LIHEAP.[2]
other_requirements: Household responsible for paying home energy costs (directly or indirectly, e.g., via rent).[3][4]
At least one adult (18+ or emancipated).[3]
At least one US citizen or qualified non-citizen.[3][4][5]
Household includes all at address sharing utility bill.[1]

benefits.value: Heating/cooling: $190 minimum to $850 maximum; Crisis: up to $2,000 maximum. Paid directly to utility vendor based on income, size, fuel type.[1]

methods: Contact local HEAT office (regional providers handle applications; see geography.offices_or_providers).[3][6]
Online eligibility check and possible application via mydoorway.utah.gov/utility-assistance.[6]
Phone or in-person at local agencies.[3][4][5]
application.processing_time: Not specified; funds limited, apply early.[1][3]
application.waitlist: No waitlist mentioned; applications stop when funds exhausted.[1][3]
forms: No specific form name/number listed; applications via local HEAT agency, possibly 873_874 for some processes.[7]
documents: Proof of ALL income for prior month for all household members (stubs, SSI, etc.).[3][4][5]
Recent utility bills (power, gas, propane, etc.).[3][4][5]
Proof of eligible medical expenses, child support, alimony (prior month).[3][4][5]
ID, SS cards for all, proof of citizenship/qualified alien status.[4]
Proof of disability or child under 6 if claiming priority.[3][4]

geography.restrictions: Administered regionally through local HEAT providers covering all Utah counties.[3]
geography.regional_variations: Different local agencies/providers per region handle applications, documents, and processing; priority access same statewide but contact local office. Tribal program (Paiute) separate, cannot duplicate state.[2][4][5]
offices_or_providers: Statewide via jobs.utah.gov/housing/scso/seal/heat.html local HEAT offices.[3]
Five County: fivecountyheat.org.[4]
MAG (Summit/Wasatch): magutah.gov/heat.[5]
Paiute Tribal (separate): pitu.gov/liheap, 435-586-1112.[2]

gotchas: Priority applications for elderly (60+), disabled, children under 6 start Oct 1; general public Nov 1; funds exhaust quickly.[1][3]
Cannot apply to both state HEAT and tribal programs.[2]
Household includes all sharing address/utility bill, unlike SNAP.[1]
Year-round but program year Oct 1-Sep 30 or funds end.[3]
Must reapply annually.[2]
programs.data_shape_notes: Administered as HEAT program under LIHEAP; regional providers required; income at 150% FPL; priority tiers for elderly/disabled/kids; benefits capped by type (heating/cooling/crisis); funds limited by exhaustion not fixed deadlines.

programs.name: Utah Weatherization Assistance Program

eligibility.income_limits: Gross annual household income at or below 200% of the Federal Poverty Level (FPL). Exact dollar amounts vary by household size and year; for example, consult current HHS Poverty Guidelines (e.g., for 2026, a single person might be around $30,120, family of 4 around $62,400—verify latest at agency). Also eligible if current HEAT program recipient (which uses 150% FPL). Income based on gross monthly without deductions.[1][2][3]
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits mentioned in program guidelines.[1][2][3]
other_requirements: Household must include at least one adult who is a U.S. citizen or qualified non-citizen.[1]
Homeowners or renters eligible; renters need notarized Weatherization Rental Agreement.[2]
Home must have never been weatherized or weatherized more than 15 years ago; determined by energy audit.[2]
Priority to elderly, disabled, households with children under 6, and high energy cost households.[2][3]

benefits.value: Professional weatherization services including computerized energy audits, advanced diagnostic equipment for cost-effective home improvements (e.g., insulation, sealing, furnace/AC/water heater repairs if crisis via HEAT), safety inspections, and indoor air quality checks. No specific dollar amount or hours; services tailored per audit.[2]

methods: Contact regional agency by county (7 agencies statewide): e.g., Bear River AoG for Box Elder/Cache/Rich (435-752-7242, weatherization@brag.utah.gov, 95 W 100 S #116, Logan UT 84321); Utah Community Action for Davis/Morgan/Weber/Salt Lake/Tooele (801-214-3215, info@utahca.org, 1307 S 900 W, Salt Lake City UT 84104); Mountainland for other areas (weatherization@mountainland.org, 478 South Geneva Rd, Vineyard UT 84059); Five County AoG for Beaver/Iron/Washington/Garfield/Kane (435-865-0195, Cedar City office).[1][2][3]
Submit by mail, email, fax, or drop-off to regional agency.
Agency service area map and full list at https://jobs.utah.gov/housing/scso/wap/how.html[1]
application.processing_time: Not specified; eligibility determined after submission, then prioritized and placed on waiting list.[1][2]
application.waitlist: Yes, applications prioritized by need (e.g., elderly, disabled) and placed on waiting list.[1][2][3]
forms: Weatherization Assistance Program application (agency-specific, e.g., downloadable from regional sites like Summit County form or UtahCA).[1][3][7]
For renters: Notarized Weatherization Rental Agreement.[2]
documents: Social security cards or HEAT approval letter.[3]
Bill History Release Authorization and copy of utility bill.[3]
Proof of ownership or owner approval (for renters).[3]
Verification of all earned/unearned income (if not on HEAT).[1][3]
All docs must be complete; name on each page for fax.[3]

geography.restrictions: Served via 7 regional agencies by county/tribal areas (e.g., Utah Community Action: Salt Lake/Tooele/Davis/Weber/Morgan; others vary).[1][3]
geography.regional_variations: Administered by different local agencies per county with own contacts, offices, fax/email/mail options; wait times/processing may vary by agency demand.[1][2][3]
offices_or_providers: Bear River AoG (Box Elder, Cache, Rich, Shoshone Tribe): Logan.[1]
Utah Community Action (Davis, Morgan, Weber, Salt Lake, Tooele, Goshute): Salt Lake City.[1][3]
Mountainland Association of Governments (e.g., Utah/Provo area): Vineyard.[2]
Five County AoG (Beaver, Iron, Washington, Garfield, Kane, Paiute): Cedar City.[1]

gotchas: Must contact specific agency by your county—statewide but regionally administered; wrong agency delays.[1]
Renters need notarized landlord agreement; HEAT recipients get priority/fast-track but must match names.[1][2][3]
Home eligibility requires audit; previously weatherized within 15 years ineligible.[2]
Priority-based waitlist favors elderly/disabled/kids under 6—others wait longer.[2][3]
U.S. citizen/qualified non-citizen adult required in household.[1]
programs.data_shape_notes: Administered by 7 county-specific agencies with unique contacts; eligibility ties to HEAT program for priority; priority tiers drive waitlist order; no age minimum but elderly prioritized.

programs.name: Utah SHIP (State Health Insurance Assistance Program)

eligibility.age: 65 or older (implied by Medicare eligibility requirement)
eligibility.income_limits: No specific income limits stated in available sources. SHIP serves all Medicare beneficiaries regardless of income, though some related programs (Medicare Savings Programs) have income limits around $1,800/month for individuals or $2,400/month for couples[5].
other_requirements: Must be eligible for Medicare or will be eligible for Medicare soon[2]
Family members and caregivers of Medicare beneficiaries can also access SHIP services[2]

benefits.value: Free, personalized one-on-one counseling and assistance. Counselors provide guidance on: Medicare terms and options, enrollment timing and procedures, Medicare Parts A, B, C, D and Medigap/supplemental insurance, Medicare appeals process, enrollment penalties, coordinating benefits, and helping with Medicare costs including prescription drugs[2]. Counselors can also help identify eligibility for Medicare Savings Programs (MSPs) and Extra Help prescription drug assistance[5].
benefits.varies_by: not_applicable — services are standardized across the program

methods: Phone: 1-800-541-7735 (Utah SHIP toll-free number)[3]
Phone: 1-877-839-2675 (National SHIP program)[2]
Online: Visit shiphelp.org, click 'Find Local Medicare Help' or 'SHIP Locator,' then select Utah[2]
In-person: Contact the Utah Department of Human Services office at 195 North 1950 West, Salt Lake City, UT 84116[3]
Email: dhotton@utah.gov[3]
Website: daas.utah.gov/seniors/[3]
application.processing_time: Not specified. Counselors suggest contacting SHIP 3 months before Medicare starts, but assistance is available at any point after that[2].
application.forms: No specific forms required — this is a counseling service, not a benefits application program
application.documents: When scheduling an appointment with local SHIP counseling office, ask what information to have ready for your specific situation. General items helpful to have include: Medicare card, insurance policies, prescription drug information, and financial documents if exploring cost-assistance programs[2].

geography.regional_variations: Not specified in available sources. SHIP operates through over 2,200 local sponsoring agencies nationwide (area agencies on aging, social services agencies, senior housing programs, hospitals)[7], but specific Utah regional office locations are not detailed.
offices_or_providers: Primary contact: Utah Department of Human Services, 195 North 1950 West, Salt Lake City, UT 84116[3]

gotchas: SHIP is a counseling and assistance program, NOT a health insurance program itself — it helps people navigate Medicare and find other programs they may qualify for[7]
SHIP can help identify eligibility for Medicare Savings Programs (MSPs) and Extra Help, but you must apply separately for those programs through Social Security Administration or other channels[5]
SHIP counselors receive 8-10 hours of intensive training on Medicare, but availability and response times may vary by local office[2]
This is different from Utah's Children's Health Insurance Program (CHIP) — SHIP is for Medicare beneficiaries (typically 65+), while CHIP serves children under 19[1][4]
Services are free and unbiased, but SHIP is an information and counseling service — it does not directly enroll you in programs or process applications for benefits
programs.data_shape_notes: Utah SHIP is a counseling and navigation service rather than a direct benefits program. It has no income or asset limits for eligibility (serves all Medicare beneficiaries), no application process (just contact to schedule counseling), and no processing timeline (it's immediate consultation). The program's value is in helping beneficiaries understand complex Medicare options and identify other assistance programs they may qualify for. Unlike CHIP, which has specific income thresholds and enrollment deadlines, SHIP is an ongoing advisory service available to all Medicare-eligible individuals.

## Key Clarifications for Your Guide

**What SHIP Actually Is:** Utah SHIP is not a health insurance program or direct financial assistance program. It's a **free counseling service** funded by the federal government to help Medicare beneficiaries and their families navigate Medicare options and identify other cost-saving programs[7]. Think of it as a personal Medicare advisor, not a benefits program.

**Who Should Contact SHIP:** Families with elderly loved ones who are 65+ and Medicare-eligible should contact SHIP to get personalized guidance on:
- Whether they qualify for Medicare Savings Programs (which help pay Medicare premiums and cost-sharing)[5]
- Whether they qualify for Extra Help with prescription drug costs[5]
- How to choose between Medicare Parts and supplemental insurance options[2]
- How to appeal Medicare decisions[2]

**The Application Process is Simple:** Unlike CHIP (which requires a formal application with income verification), SHIP requires just a phone call or online contact to schedule a counseling appointment. There's no waiting period or eligibility determination — you simply call and speak with a trained counselor[2][3].

**One Important Limitation:** The search results do not provide specific information about asset limits, detailed regional office locations throughout Utah, or specific processing timelines for related programs that SHIP helps with. For a complete guide, you may need to contact Utah SHIP directly at 1-800-541-7735[3] to gather additional operational details.

programs.name: Utah Meals on Wheels

eligibility.income_limits: No income limits; program is available regardless of income[6].
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits mentioned across sources.
other_requirements: Homebound (unable to leave home without assistance)[1][5][6][8]
Unable to shop or prepare meals due to mobility challenges, disability, or frailty[1][2][5][7]
Resident of the local program's delivery zone or service area (varies by county/PSA)[1][5]
Do not drive a vehicle (in some areas like Davis County)[5]
Priority to isolated individuals[6][8]
Spouses or disabled dependents may qualify in some programs[1][6]
Under 60 may qualify in limited cases (e.g., disabilities, alternatives clients, or spouses/children with payment)[3][6]

benefits.value: Nutritious home-delivered midday/lunch meals (hot, one-third daily nutritional needs, low-sodium, heart-healthy; includes protein, vegetables, fruit, grains, milk); 1 meal per delivery day, 5 days/week (Mon-Fri, excluding holidays); optional weekend meals (delivered Thu/Fri in some areas); frequency 1-5 days/week based on need; personal wellness check during delivery[3][5][6][7][8]

methods: Contact local Area Agency on Aging (AAA), senior center, or Meals on Wheels provider by phone or online referral[1][2][6]
Phone examples: Davis County (801-525-5099 implied via site), Utah/Wasatch/Summit Counties (801-229-3802), Cache County (435-755-1720 or 435-755-1722)[5][6][7]
Online referral (e.g., MAG Utah: magutah.gov/meals/)[6]
In-person assessment required by staff before starting[6][7]
application.processing_time: Varies; some within a week, others longer due to waitlists; initial assessment after contact[1]
application.waitlist: Yes, in some areas (e.g., Utah County since Jan 2022)[6]
forms: Nutrition screen (optional, not required to deny meal; may be needed for liquid/medical meals)[3]
No specific statewide form name; local assessment questions on age, health, mobility, dietary needs, emergency contacts[1]
documents: Proof of age, residence, medical information (for homebound/medical meals); financial docs sometimes but income not always required[1][3]

geography.restrictions: Limited to delivery zones/service areas within local PSAs/counties; must verify local coverage[1][3]
geography.regional_variations: Administered locally by AAAs/senior centers with differences in: providers, phone numbers, costs ($3.50-$4 suggested donation/meal, voluntary; $6 for under 60 at centers), weekend meals, waitlists (e.g., Utah County), exact eligibility nuances (e.g., no driving in Davis), delivery frequency[3][5][6][7]
offices_or_providers: Davis County Aging Services[5]
Mountainland Assoc. of Govts (MAG) for Utah/Wasatch/Summit Counties[6]
Cache County Senior Center[7]
Local AAAs per region[1][3]

programs.official_source: https://rules.utah.gov/publicat/code_rtf/r510-104.rtf (Utah Admin Code R510-104, Aging & Adult Services)
gotchas: Must live in specific delivery zone; check local program first[1]
Homebound strictly assessed/documented annually; able to leave easily or have caregivers may not qualify[1][3][5]
Suggested donations ($3.50-$4/meal) are voluntary but help expand service; under 60 often pay full[5][6]
Meals not left if client absent; must cancel by 9am[7]
Waitlists in high-demand areas like Utah County[6]
Car ownership may disqualify in some areas[1][5]
In-person assessment mandatory before starting[6][7]
programs.data_shape_notes: Decentralized by county/PSA with local AAAs managing; no statewide income/asset tests; variations in costs, waitlists, exact rules, and providers; homebound + 60+ core but priority to isolated/frail

programs.name: Utah Caregiver Support Program (UCSP)

eligibility.age: Caregiver must be 18 or older (with priority given to caregivers 55+); care recipient must be 60+ OR any age with Alzheimer's Disease or related neurological disorder
other_requirements: Caregiver must be an unpaid, informal family member or other individual providing in-home and community care[1][2]
Caregiver must demonstrate medium-to-high risk score on DAAS Approved Demographic Intake and Screening tool[3]
Caregiver must complete DAAS-approved Assessment and Caregiver Burden score[3]
Care recipient must have deficits in at least two activities of daily living[5]
Caregiver must demonstrate a level of stress[5]
Special eligibility for older relatives (55+) serving as primary caregivers to children under 18 or adults 18-59 with disabilities when biological/adoptive parents cannot serve[1][2]

benefits.value: Up to $1,500 in services over a 12-month period[1]; services include respite care, case management, personal care aides, adult day care, overnight respite, information & referral services, caregiver support groups, counseling, caregiver education/training[1][2]

methods: Contact Local Area Agency on Aging (specific offices vary by county)[2]
Salt Lake County: 801-468-2460[5]
Davis County: 801-451-3377[5]
Weber/Morgan Counties (Care for the Caregiver Program): 801-625-3866[5]
Online: https://daas.utah.gov/seniors/[2]
Main program website: https://magutah.gov/cgsupport/[1]
application.processing_time: Not specified in available sources; program often has a waiting list[5]
application.waitlist: Yes, program often maintains a waiting list; applicants served in priority order using DAAS-approved Demographic Intake and Risk Screening tool and Caregiver Burden score[3][5]
forms: DAAS Approved Demographic Intake and Screening tool[3]
application.documents: Not specified in available sources; recommend contacting local Area Agency on Aging for specific intake requirements

geography.restrictions: Administered through Local Area Agencies on Aging by county[2]
geography.regional_variations: Program is coordinated statewide but delivered through county-based Area Agencies on Aging; phone numbers and contact points vary by county (Salt Lake, Davis, Weber/Morgan specifically listed)[5]
offices_or_providers: Salt Lake County Aging & Adult Services: 801-468-2460
Davis County: 801-451-3377
Weber/Morgan Counties: 801-625-3866
Contact DAAS for other counties: https://daas.utah.gov/seniors/

gotchas: No income requirement means eligibility is broader than many assume, but caregiver MUST demonstrate medium-to-high caregiver burden/stress — this is not automatic[3][5]
Services capped at $1,500 per 12-month period — this is a significant limitation for ongoing care needs[1]
Program often has a waiting list; applicants are prioritized by burden score and demographic factors, not first-come-first-served[3][5]
Care recipient must have deficits in at least TWO activities of daily living — single-activity limitations may not qualify[5]
Caregiver must be unpaid/informal — paid caregivers or facility staff do not qualify[1][2]
No fees charged, but donations encouraged — this is a low-cost program but not necessarily comprehensive[1]
Priority is given to older caregivers with greatest social/economic need; younger caregivers may face longer waits[3]
Different county offices may have different wait times and service availability — contact your specific county Area Agency on Aging for current status
programs.data_shape_notes: This program is unique in having NO income limits but STRICT burden/stress requirements. Benefits are capped at $1,500/year (not unlimited). Eligibility is determined through a multi-step assessment process (intake screening + burden score) rather than simple demographic criteria. Program is statewide but county-administered, creating potential regional variation in wait times and service availability. Priority tiers exist based on age and economic need, not first-come-first-served. Care recipient must meet functional criteria (2+ ADL deficits), not just age/diagnosis.

    "Program often has a waiting list; applicants are prioritized by burden score and demographic factors, not first-come-first-served[3][5]",

Program is statewide but county-administered, creating potential regional variation in wait times and service availability. ## Key Takeaways for Families

**The biggest gotcha:** This program has no income limits, which sounds generous, but eligibility hinges on demonstrating medium-to-high caregiver burden through a formal assessment[3][5]. You can't simply apply because your parent is 60+; you must prove you're under significant stress as a caregiver.

**The financial reality:** The program caps benefits at $1,500 per year[1]. This is respite and support services, not ongoing paid care. It's designed to provide temporary relief and delay facility placement, not replace full-time caregiving.

**The wait:** The program maintains a waiting list[5]. Applicants are prioritized by caregiver burden score and demographic factors (older caregivers with greater economic need go first)[3]. Processing time is not publicly specified, so contact your county Area Agency on Aging for current wait estimates.

**The application path:** You must contact your county's Area Agency on Aging, not apply directly online[2]. County-specific phone numbers are available above. They will conduct an intake screening and administer the formal Caregiver Burden assessment to determine eligibility[3].

**What you actually get:** Respite care (temporary substitute care), case management, personal care aides, adult day care, overnight respite, support groups, counseling, and caregiver training[1][2]. The specific mix depends on your assessed needs and available county resources.

programs.name: Utah Legal Services for Seniors

eligibility.income_limits: Income must be at or below 125% of the federal poverty guidelines (e.g., in 2012: $13,613 annually for a 1-person household, $27,938 for a family of four). Exact current dollar amounts vary by year and household size; check current federal poverty guidelines via ULS[3][4].

other_requirements: Legal issues must relate to senior-specific problems like public benefits (Social Security, SSI, Medicare, Medicaid), adult protective services (abuse, guardianship, powers-of-attorney), housing, consumer issues, living wills[4][5][6].

benefits.value: Free legal assistance including counseling, representation, document preparation, and preventive legal presentations on topics like public benefits, housing, consumer issues, Medicare claims, elder abuse, guardianship, powers-of-attorney, wills, estates. Specialized Senior Citizen Law Center staff; volunteer attorneys for wills/estates via Senior Law Project[4][5][6]. No specific dollar amounts or hours stated.

I need to clarify an important distinction: **the Utah Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program is not a benefits or services program for elderly individuals—it is an advocacy program for residents already living in long-term care facilities.**

Based on the search results, this program does not have eligibility requirements, income limits, asset limits, or an application process in the way you've structured your question. Here's what the program actually is:

## What This Program Actually Does

The Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program **advocates on behalf of residents** who already live in nursing homes, assisted living facilities, skilled rehab facilities, and memory care facilities.[2][4] Ombudsmen investigate complaints, resolve problems, and educate families about residents' rights.[6] **Services are free and confidential, provided without regard to income or resources.**[2]

This is fundamentally different from a program that provides benefits or services to help someone enter or pay for long-term care.

programs.name: Utah Aging Waiver

eligibility.income_limits: Single applicant limit is $1,305/month (effective 3/1/25–2/28/26) or $1,330/month (effective March 2026–Feb 2027); limits increase annually in March and apply to individual income with special deductions for living expenses and exemption of spousal income even if living together; almost all income counted[1][2][3].
eligibility.asset_limits: Single applicant: $2,000 in countable assets; exempt: primary home (equity under $730,000 if applicant or spouse resides there or intends to return), one automobile (any value), household furnishings/appliances, personal effects/belongings, burial plots/irrevocable burial trusts up to $7,000 each, life insurance face value ≤$1,500; 60-month look-back rule applies with penalty period for transfers below fair market value[1][2][3].
other_requirements: Utah resident and U.S. citizen or qualified immigrant[2][4].

Nursing Facility Level of Care (NFLOC) determined by RN using InterRAI MDS-HC tool: requires 2 of 3 conditions—significant physical assistance with ≥2 ADLs (toileting, bathing, dressing, transferring, mobility, eating), poor orientation requiring NF care, or medical condition unmet safely without waiver services; dementia diagnosis alone insufficient[1][3][4].

benefits.value: Core: case management; other services include personal care, respite/respite care (including LTC facility extension >13 days), adult day care, home modifications/environmental adaptations, specialized medical equipment/supplies/assistive technology (no physician prescription needed for some), non-medical transportation, supplemental meals, personal emergency response systems (PERS) installation/testing/removal/purchase/rental/repair/response, training services, personal budget assistance; no fixed dollar/hour limits specified, tailored via case manager plan of care[4][6].

methods: Phone intake with local Aging & Adult Services office (e.g., Salt Lake County or San Juan County); statewide via Utah Medicaid[4][5].
In-person: local county aging services offices (e.g., San Juan County, Salt Lake County)[4][5].
application.waitlist: Not mentioned; applications accepted anytime (waiver allows continuous enrollment outside tri-annual periods)[6].
forms: No specific form name/number listed; begins with phone intake/assessment by RN/case manager[1][4][5].
documents: Proof of income, assets, residency, citizenship/immigration status, medical records/doctor documentation for NFLOC assessment.
Financial details for asset/income verification[1][2][3].

geography.restrictions: None; available statewide for Utah residents age 65+[2][4].
geography.regional_variations: Administered via local county aging services (e.g., San Juan County, Salt Lake County) with potential differences in providers/case managers, but core eligibility/benefits uniform[4][5].
offices_or_providers: Local Aging & Adult Services offices by county, in partnership with Utah Medicaid[4][5].

60-month look-back penalty for asset transfers; spousal income exempt even if cohabiting, but separate asset formula[1][4].

Distinguish from New Choices Waiver (higher income limit $2,901/month, different target group) or ABD Medicaid[2][3].
